summary: Victim > Foolish jam shines amid uneven first set
blurb: The second‑set Victim Or The Crime → Foolish Heart sequence is a high‑energy highlight, with Jerry locked in and Vince delivering strong keyboards on “It’s All Too Much.” First‑set classics such as “Greatest Story Ever Told” and “Bertha” are solid, though several numbers fall flat.
review: June 27, 1995 at The Palace offered a mixed bag of moments that still reward a focused listen. Jerry Garcia was largely on fire, especially during the Victim Or The Crime → Foolish Heart stretch where his improvisation lifted the band into a tight, soaring jam that many fans cite as the show’s high point. Vince Welnick added sparkle with a confident performance on “It’s All Too Much,” and the rhythm section kept a steady drive throughout. The first set delivered reliable staples – “Greatest Story Ever Told,” “Bertha,” and “New Minglewood Blues” – but suffered from weaker renditions of “Ramble On Rose,” “Queen Jane Approximately,” and “Lazy River Road.” The second set recovered, closing with an uplifting “Sugar Magnolia” and a brief encore of “Liberty.” While the show isn’t flawless, its standout moments—particularly the Victim/Foolish jam—make it worth revisiting for Deadheads seeking that 1995 tour spark.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are positive, two mixed; consensus praises the Victim > Foolish jam and Jerry’s locked‑in playing while noting uneven first‑set numbers. Positive language (“locked in,” “great performance”) outweighs the mixed criticism, yielding a modest +1.0 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear Nak300 audience tape captures Jerry’s lock‑in and the Victim > Foolish jam with excellent fidelity, making it the most listenable source.

key_highlights: Victim Or The Crime → Foolish Heart sequence delivers a tight, soaring jam.
Vince Welnick’s keyboards shine on “It’s All Too Much.”
First‑set classics “Greatest Story Ever Told” and “Bertha” are solid.
"Sugar Magnolia" closes the second set with uplifting energy.

band_performance.Jerry: Locked‑in for most of the show, especially soaring during the Victim > Foolish jam; some critics note occasional loss of focus early on.
band_performance.Vince: Provides strong keyboard work and vocal harmonies, highlighted on “It’s All Too Much.”
band_performance.Billy: Drums are solid and energetic throughout, typical of the 1995 tour.
